nativeRange.habitat: Warm, slow-moving, tannin-stained freshwater bodies of the Amazon basin — backwaters, oxbow lakes, blackwater streams, swamps, and seasonal flood pools.
nativeRange.biotope: Amazon blackwater / leaf-litter pool, low-flow with abundant submerged wood and detritus.

decorPreferences: broad-leaved or floating plants for cover

diet.notes: Suction-feeding ambush predator. Generally advised to offer earthworms, nightcrawlers, blackworms, krill, and feeder/frozen fish. Lacks teeth and tongue — captures prey by rapid mouth expansion. Will attempt to eat anything that fits in its mouth, including tankmates.

avoidWith: any tankmate small enough to be ambushed

summary: A fully aquatic, flattened South American frog famous for the female's back-brooding strategy — eggs are embedded into her dorsal skin and froglets emerge directly from her back.
careNotes: Generally considered a niche specialist species rather than a typical hobby amphibian. Surinam Toads are large (commonly 10–15 cm, females reaching ~17 cm), fully aquatic, and helpless on land — the entire enclosure must be aquatic with a securely fitting lid (they have been known to push through loose-fitting covers). They are obligate ambush predators that will consume essentially any tankmate small enough to swallow, so a species-only setup is strongly advised. Water is generally kept warm (~23–26°C) and on the soft, slightly acidic side; their thin skin is sensitive to chlorine, copper, and other contaminants, so dechlorinated water and gentle 25–50% water changes are routinely recommended. Filtration should provide turnover without strong current. Avoid sand and fine gravel — large smooth river rocks or a bare bottom reduce impaction risk during feeding strikes. Trade availability is limited; most specimens offered in the hobby are wild-caught and may arrive stressed or parasitised, so quarantine and conservative handling are advised. Cross-source disagreement: minimum tank-size figures from hobby sources range from ~110 L (29 gal) for a pair to 150 L+ (40 gal) for a single adult; we have chosen 150 L as a cautious-but-reasonable floor given the species' size and predatory nature.
breedingNotes: Famous for one of the most distinctive reproductive strategies in vertebrates: during amplexus the pair performs a series of underwater somersaults, and the male presses the fertilised eggs onto the female's back, where they sink into her thickened dorsal skin. Eggs are progressively enveloped by a honeycomb of skin pockets, and after roughly 3–5 months fully-formed froglets emerge directly from the mother's back. Captive breeding has been documented but is generally considered difficult; deep water (around 1.2 m has been reported) is typically advised to allow the somersaulting amplexus rise. Froglets are cannibalistic on emergence and should be separated and grown out individually or in size-matched groups.

primary.name: Reptiles Magazine
primary.notes: Used as primary because the manifest-supplied Josh's Frogs root returned HTTP 403 and Josh's Frogs catalogues only Pipa parva (Sabana Suriname Toad), not Pipa pipa. Reptiles Magazine 'Caring For The Surinam Underwater Toad' is a long-form hobbyist husbandry article covering temperature, breeding behaviour, diet, and tankmate compatibility for P. pipa.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Pipidae, Anura), native range, adult size (males to ~15.4 cm, females to ~17.1 cm), habitat description, and back-brooding reproductive biology.

additional.name: Reptile Apartment
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (cited 25.5–26°C, pH ~7.0–7.2), tank size (~150 L / 40 gal for a single adult), substrate guidance (large smooth rocks; avoid sand/gravel due to impaction risk), captive lifespan (~6 years), and predatory feeding behaviour.

additional.name: Josh's Frogs (manifest-supplied primary, unreachable)
additional.notes: Manifest-supplied primary source root. Returned HTTP 403 to the fetch tool. Independent checks indicate Josh's Frogs stocks Pipa parva (Sabana Suriname Toad), a smaller congener, rather than Pipa pipa, so even a successful fetch would not have provided species-specific husbandry data. Primary role swapped to Reptiles Magazine per playbook §3 fallback rule.

amphibian.breedingNotes: Females incubate eggs in honeycomb-like pockets within their dorsal skin; fully-formed froglets emerge from the mother's back after roughly 3–5 months. Captive breeding has been reported but is generally regarded as difficult and is commonly associated with deep tanks (~1.2 m water column) that allow the species' characteristic somersaulting amplexus.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Large (10–17 cm), fully aquatic ambush predator with limited captive husbandry literature and limited hobby trade availability; will consume any tankmate small enough to swallow and requires a species-only setup with carefully buffered, soft acidic water.
